data.title: UK ambassador said America's only 'special relationship' is with Israel: Report
data.description:
U.K. Ambassador to the U.S. Christian Turner said in February that America’s only “special relationship” is with Israel, not his country, according to the Financial Times.

“I think there is probably one country that has a special relationship with the United States — and that is probably Israel,” Turner told a group of U.K. students visiting Washington, D.C., the outlet reported Tuesday.

Turner also said that calling ties between the U.S. and U.K. a “special relationship,” a term first coined by former British Prime Minister Winston Churchill, is “nostalgic” and “backwards-looking.”

The ambassador, who was appointed by British Prime Minister Keir Starmer to the post in February, acknowledged the “deep history and affinity” between the two countries that same month.

“Particularly on defense and security, we are intertwined,” he noted. 

“The relationship will carry on, if you want, being ‘special’, but I think it’s going to have to be different.” Turner later remarked.

Whether it is “special” or not, the U.S.-U.K. partnership is under the microscope this week amid King Charles III and Queen Camilla’s state visit to Washington. The king is set to address a joint meeting of Congress on Tuesday at 3 p.m. EDT.

President Trump has frequently criticized Starmer in recent months, particularly for not militarily backing the U.S.-Israeli war in Iran and electing not to assist in efforts to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

But on Tuesday, the president touted the “unique bond” between the U.S. and U.K. He even described the relationship as a “special” one.

“Beneath those beautiful flags eight decades ago, Prime Minister Winston Churchill and President Franklin Roosevelt famously met on a ship in the North Atlantic to outline a vision for the free world after World War II,” he said at the White House, with Charles looking on. 

“That understanding of our nations’ unique bond and role in history is the essence of our special relationship, and we hope it will always remain that way.”

U.K. Foreign Minister Yvette Cooper also said Monday that while the governments of the two countries may disagree from time to time, the bond remains.

“The fact that we have such a deep, longstanding relationship means that actually there are things that throughout our history of going back for 250 years, there are things, of course, that we have disagreed on, but there’s also, I think, that shared respect,” she told host Jake Tapper on CNN’s “The Lead.”

data.title: Cases of drug-resistant salmonella, several among kids, linked to outbreak; CDC issues warning
data.description:
(NEXSTAR) – The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention is warning of more than two dozen cases of possibly drug-resistant salmonella cases across 13 states.

In an update last week, the CDC said all 34 people — several of whom are children— have the same strain of salmonella, which has been linked to backyard poultry.

To make matters more difficult, the CDC says an analysis of all 34 patients showed the strain may be resistant to the antibiotic fosfomycin. Samples from eight of those patients showed there could be resistance to other antibiotics commonly used in salmonella infections.

Here’s what to know.

Where have the drug-resistant cases of salmonella been found?

So far, cases have primarily been found in the Midwest. As of Tuesday, the CDC shows Michigan has reported the most confirmed cases at six, followed by Ohio and Wisconsin, which each have had five cases.

Indiana, Kentucky, and Maine have each had three confirmed cases, while Maryland and West Virginia have recorded two each.

Florida, Illinois, Mississippi, New Hampshire, and Tennessee have each confirmed a single case.

What do we know about the patients?

The patients range in age from less than a year old to 78 years old, with the median age being 12 years old.

According to the CDC, roughly a dozen of those who became ill are under the age of 5.

Thirteen of those who fell ill required hospitalization. The CDC warns, though, that there could be more sick people in the same outbreak. Cases have so far been reported between late February and the end of March.

Health officials interviewed 29 patients, with 23 saying they had contact with backyard poultry in the week before becoming ill.

Fourteen people said they own backyard birds, with all but one saying they got new poultry since the start of the year. The CDC said investigators are working to determine if there is a common source of the poultry.

What to know about the salmonella infections

While the CDC has linked the above cases to backyard poultry, it’s not clear which species in particular is to blame. The agency notes that any backyard birds, including ducks and chickens, can carry the bacteria.

You don’t need to touch the poultry to be exposed, either. Touching anything in their environment, then touching your mouth or food without washing your hands, can expose you to the salmonella germs.

Salmonella infections are commonly associated with diarrhea, fever and stomach cramps, according to the CDC. Symptoms can begin between six hours and six days after you’ve ingested the bacteria. Most people are able to recover without receiving treatment within four to seven days.

Illnesses may be more severe for young children, the elderly, and those with weakened immune systems. Some may require medical treatment or hospitalization, the CDC explains.

Infections can only be diagnosed with a laboratory test of a person’s stool, body tissue or fluids.

To protect yourself, the CDC encourages washing your hands after touching birds, anything in their habitat, and their eggs. Don’t wear the shoes or boots you use in the birds’ area inside your home, and keep the poultry and their supplies outside.

Children younger than 5 should not handle the birds or their materials, as they are more likely to fall ill from salmonella, the CDC adds.

Every year, salmonella causes roughly 1.35 million illnesses and 420 deaths.

data.title: Feds conduct Minnesota raids related to alleged welfare fraud
data.description:
Law enforcement agencies are executing multiple search warrants in Minnesota on Tuesday in connection with alleged fraud in the state’s welfare programs, according to federal officials.    

“Homeland Security Investigations in cooperation with our law enforcement partners executed criminal search warrants in Minneapolis relating to the rampant fraud of U.S. taxpayers dollars,” the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) shared in a post on social platform X.

Raids were conducted on 22 locations across the Twin Cities area on Tuesday morning, mostly targeting child and daycare providers that receive Medicaid funding, sources told Nexstar’s KARE 11.

The search warrants were reportedly not related to any immigration enforcement operation.

“Today the FBI with federal, state and local law enforcement is involved in court-authorized law enforcement activity as part of an ongoing fraud investigation,” the Justice Department (DOJ) said in a statement to NewsNation, The Hill’s sister network.

The Trump administration dispatched federal authorities to Minnesota in early January to investigate what officials described as “rampant fraud” in the state’s social services program. It came on the heels of a sweeping federal probe in which dozens of people have been charged.

President Trump claimed in November that Minnesota had become a “hub of fraudulent money laundering activity,” tying the allegation to Somali immigrants living in the state.   

Sources told Fox News reporter Bill Melugin on Tuesday that the locations targeted in the morning raids were largely Somali-linked businesses, including the “Quality Learning Center” daycare.

“The task force and the DOJ will be relentless in exposing these fraudsters wherever they may be hiding,” Vice President Vance wrote on X.

Trump administration officials announced in February that hundreds of millions of dollars in Medicaid funding to Minnesota would be temporarily halted over fraud concerns, a move Gov. Tim Walz (D) slammed as “political punishment.”

Walz, who dropped his bid for reelection in January amid mounting scrutiny over his handling of the fraud scandal, appeared supportive of the raids on Tuesday.

“If you commit fraud in Minnesota you’re going to get caught — and that’s exactly what we saw today,” he wrote on X. “We catch criminals when state and federal agencies share information. Joint investigations work, and securing justice depends on it.”

data.title: While America fights a war, the State Department is focused on dress codes
data.description:
The U.S. is more than a month into an active war with Iran. The State Department’s response has included telling its diplomats what to wear. 

That choice, more than the policy itself, should concern Congress. At a moment when the U.S. needs a diplomatic corps built for crisis, the department is signaling something else: Appearance is getting attention that capability is not.  

This new dress code didn’t happen in a vacuum. Only days earlier, the department rolled out changes to how it recruits and evaluates America’s diplomats. Restarting hiring after months of disruption is necessary. The Foreign Service has lost more than 20 percent of its workforce since January 2025. But how that rebuilding happens matters just as much as whether it happens at all.

One change stands out: the removal of an assessment tool that helped identify candidates with the hardest-to-replace skills. That includes real-world international experience and critical languages like Chinese, Arabic and Farsi. There has been no clear explanation for why it was cut. Given current global tensions, the decision is difficult to defend.

The administration has argued it wants diplomats who are accountable to American interests, not to a professional foreign policy establishment it views with suspicion. But if the goal is to advance American interests in Iran, across the Gulf and throughout the world, we need diplomats who understand those regions, speak those languages and have spent years developing judgment under pressure. 

As president of the American Foreign Service Association, the union and professional association of the Foreign Service, my concern is that the real risk isn’t that ideology once influenced the system — it is that it will now. 

“America First” should entail a Foreign Service built for effectiveness. We need diplomats with deep expertise, independent judgment and the ability to operate in complex environments. What’s taking shape instead looks increasingly like a system that rewards compliance. 

That shift is already visible. Experienced officers — people with decades of regional knowledge and crisis experience — have left or been pushed out. These are not skills you can replace quickly. At the same time, key pipelines have been weakened. The Diplomats in Residence program at key universities has been shut down. The Pickering and Rangel fellowships, longstanding entry points for top-tier, diverse talent, have been disrupted. Reductions in force have hollowed out Middle East expertise during an active conflict in that region.

Meanwhile, vital regional embassies — Egypt, Iraq, Kuwait, Pakistan, Qatar, Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates — are operating without Senate-confirmed ambassadors. In each of these cases, there isn’t even a nominee pending, meaning no high-level U.S. representation for months to come.

That has real consequences. Confirmed ambassadors bring authority and relationships with host governments that acting officials cannot fully replicate, particularly during a crisis. 

The pattern extends beyond staffing. When former ambassadors, career diplomats who served under both Republican and Democratic administrations, raised concerns last month about American safety during evacuations from the conflict, the response from a senior State Department official was a personal attack.

These are individuals with firsthand experience managing evacuations and navigating crises like this one. If they are dismissed when they speak up, the message to those still serving is clear: stay quiet. Oversight depends on people being willing to surface problems. If they’re discouraged from doing so, Congress will hear less of what it needs to know. 

The dress code fits into this broader pattern. Policies like this are rarely just about clothing. When written loosely — and this one is — they are often applied unevenly, with disproportionate impact on women and underrepresented groups. That is not necessarily by intent, but because the policy was never fully vetted.

Taken together, the trajectory is clear. Narrow who gets in. Weaken the systems that build expertise. Push out those who have it. Undercut dissent. Skip internal checks. Focus on appearance.

That is not how you strengthen an institution. 

Members of the House Foreign Affairs Committee and Senate Foreign Relations Committee should press for answers: How will the new hiring system be protected from political interference? What is the plan to fill ambassadorial vacancies in active conflict zones? And what is being done to bring back experienced officers whose skills are urgently needed? 

These are questions that get to whether the United States has the diplomatic capacity this moment demands. China does. Shouldn’t we? 

The State Department can tell its diplomats what to wear. It cannot obscure what is happening to the institution they serve. 

John Dinkelman is a 37-year veteran of the U.S. State Department, where he held senior leadership roles worldwide and in Washington, including acting assistant secretary of state for administration.

data.title: Southern Poverty Law Center: Informant program was not kept secret
data.description:
The Southern Poverty Law Center (SPLC) refuted the federal government’s allegation that the civil rights organization obscured details about its paid informant program in new court filings on Tuesday, following last week’s grand jury indictment.

In two motions submitted to a federal court on Tuesday, the SPLC pushed back on acting U.S. Attorney General Todd Blanche’s claim that the nonprofit paid leaders of extremist groups like the Ku Klux Klan and did not share information from these informants with the federal government. 

A federal grand jury indicted the Montgomery, Alabama-based organization on April 21 on six counts of wire fraud, four counts of bank fraud and one count of conspiracy to commit money laundering. 

The SPLC called the indictment a “stunning and unremitting departure from Justice Department policy and established law” in one of its Tuesday motions. The group cited its “decades” of cooperation with the FBI. 

“This prosecution is as unprecedented as it is irregular,” the civil rights organization wrote. “Comments made by Administration officials and flaws in the indictment itself suggest the case results from misleading both the public and the grand jury that voted on the charges.”

The SPLC has paid informants within designated hate organizations in the past as part of efforts to detect and prevent violence led by these groups, according to a Tuesday press release from the civil rights organization. 

The Justice Department argued in its indictment last week that the SPLC paid an organizer of the 2017 “Unite the Right” event in Charlottesville, Virginia, while that member made racist posts and coordinated transportation for participants. 

“In order to covertly pay its field sources, the SPLC opened bank accounts connected to a series of fictitious entities,” the DOJ said in the indictment. “The covert nature of the accounts allowed the SPLC to disguise the true nature, source, ownership, and control of the fraudulently obtained donated money the SPLC paid the field sources.”

The SPLC responded to these allegations on Tuesday, saying the organization shared a 45-page “event alert” with the FBI ahead of the Charlottesville rally that included details from paid informants.

The FBI led the investigation into the SPLC, with assistance from the Internal Revenue Service Criminal Investigation. 

The SPLC requested the full transcript from the grand jury proceedings and asked that Blanche and other government officials retract their “materially false statements” about the civil rights organization.

The SPLC’s interim president and CEO, Bryan Fair, said in the Tuesday press release that the organization has “saved lives” with the information it obtained and shared with the FBI through this program over the past 40 years.

“When we began working with informants, we were living in the shadow of the height of the Civil Rights Movement, which had seen bombings at churches, state-sponsored violence against demonstrators and the murders of activists that went unanswered by the justice system,” Fair said. “When threats and other unlawful activity were revealed, the SPLC immediately passed that information to law enforcement officials, local, state and federal and assisted in efforts to prevent violence and stop criminal activity.”

data.title: Gas prices at highest level since beginning of Iran war
data.description:
The average cost of gas in the U.S. on Tuesday reached its highest level in four years — a result of skyrocketing energy prices triggered by the U.S.-Israeli conflict with Iran.

The national average reached $4.18, a 40-percent jump since the conflict began on Feb. 28, according to data from AAA. States in the West have seen some of the highest average gas prices, with California having the highest average at $5.97. Oklahoma holds the lowest average price of gas at $3.57.

Gas prices have not reached these high levels since April 2022, when Russia invaded Ukraine and the national average in the U.S. peaked at $4.11. This average reappeared on Monday before bumping up by 7 cents.

West Texas Intermediate (WTI) futures, the U.S. benchmark, rose to over $99 per barrel on Tuesday, and Brent crude jumped to over $111 per barrel.

More than three-quarters of Americans blame President Trump for the surge in gas prices, according to a Reuters/Ipsos poll released on Friday. About 77 percent said he bears at least some responsibility, while 22 percent disagreed.

Prices leaped after Iran shut down the Strait of Hormuz in the days following the start of the conflict. Iran reopened the strait twice only to briefly shut it down again, leaving the path of transit closed off to oil tankers transporting roughly 20 percent of the world’s oil and gas.

Trump said Tuesday that Iran told the U.S. to reopen the strait, despite previously deploying a Navy fleet blockading ships seeking to enter the strait.

According to the president, Iran wants the U.S. “to ‘Open the Hormuz Strait,’ as soon as possible, as they try to figure out their leadership situation (Which I believe they will be able to do!)” he wrote online. Trump did not elaborate on who told him this or if the administration will comply.

Iranian officials submitted a proposal to end the war and reopen the strait, but it would also hold off on any discussions about Iran’s nuclear program, The Associated Press reported, citing two regional officials. Secretary of State Marco Rubio derided this last portion of the proposal.

“There’s no doubt in my mind that at some point in the future, if this radical clerical regime remains in charge in Iran, they will decide they want a nuclear weapon,” Rubio told Fox News’s Trey Yingst. “That fundamental issue still has to be confronted. That still remains the core issue here.”

The Trump administration has defended its military offensive as a means to prevent Iran from being an “imminent nuclear threat.” The Islamic Republic has rejected these claims, arguing that its nuclear program was designed for peaceful purposes.

data.title: Trump jokes his mother had a crush on King Charles
data.description:
President Trump joked Tuesday that his mother, Mary MacLeod, used to have a crush on young King Charles III.

Trump, during his remarks on the White House’s South Lawn during the arrival ceremony of King Charles and Queen Camila, said his mother “loved the royal family and she loved the Queen.” 

“Any time the Queen was involved in the ceremony or anything, my mother would be glued to the television,” he said. “And should say, ‘Look, Donald, look how beautiful that is.'”

“But I also remember her saying very clearly, ‘Charles, look, young Charles. He’s so cute,'” he said. “My mother had a crush on Charles. Can you believe it? I wonder what she’s thinking right now.”

Trump spoke of his family’s lineage, particularlyy his mother who, was born in Scotland and came to the U.S. at age 19.

She was born in Stornoway, Scotland in the Outer Hebrides, Trump said.

“That’s what they call very serious Scotland. There’s no question about it,” he said. “Some places they say, ‘Well, it wasn’t really Scotland,’ the Hebrides, that’s real serious Scotland. That’s where they had their greatest of warriors.”

Trump said his mother and father, Fred Trump, were married for 63 years, and joked to first lady Melania Trump, that they couldn’t match that.

“And excuse me, if you don’t mind, that’s a record we won’t be able to match, darling, sorry, just not going to work out that way,” he said. “We’ll do well, but we’re not going to do that well. 63 years.”

Trump’s speech was part of the official state ceremony greeting the king and queen. They will also participate in a gift exchange and guestbook signing.

The president and the King will then have a bilateral meeting, while the first lady and Queen Camilla will engage with students who are learning the history about U.S. and the United Kingdom.

King Charles is also expected to address a joint meeting of Congress later Tuesday.

data.title: Cases of drug-resistant salmonella, several among kids, linked to outbreak; CDC issues warning
data.description:

data.title: We need AI, but the political backlash cannot be ignored
data.description:
Artificial Intelligence holds the promise to improve life on the planet almost beyond description, such as developing new cancer therapies, but breakneck development of the data centers that run AI (along with more mundane applications from gambling to porn to cat videos) has spurred a grass-roots political backlash that is growing and could be a major factor in this year’s elections.

If dismissed by political and corporate leaders, the backlash threatens to result in extreme measures such as the ban on data center development just passed by the legislature of Maine, which Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.) and Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (D-N.Y.) want to impose nationally.

Local rejection of data center projects is growing rapidly. Virginia, the “Data Center Alley” of concentrated development, reflects the magnitude of the backlash. A recent Washington Post poll showed that Virginians’ support for data centers locating in their communities dropped from 69 percent just three years ago to only 35 percent now. The precipitous drop in support for data centers cuts across all voters, Democratic, Republican and independent, and in all areas of the state, urban, suburban and rural.

The backlash on the Bernie-AOC left may be rooted in ideological rage against corporate wealth and power, epitomized by Big Tech companies which are now the richest and most powerful in history. But hostility is growing on the right as well, reflected in Florida Republican Gov. Ron DeSantis’s (R) proposals to restrict data centers.

Much of the growing opposition comes from a realization that the rapid development of data centers, especially those running AI, has pushed the power grid to the precipice of a real crisis of reliability. Data centers use huge amounts of power, and the unprecedented growth in power demand has far exceeded the ability of the power grid to add new power supply resources quickly enough to prevent power shortfalls.

And data centers typically require new wires and generation, both of which are extremely costly. As consumers’ power bills rise relentlessly and politicians respond to voter ire over their bills, data centers have become a convenient villain.

Further fueling the backlash are politically tone-deaf proposals such as the Trump administration’s directive to the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission to take an unprecedented action: claiming authority to override the states over how and when data centers connect to the power grid. The purposes of this proposal, being pushed by the industry, are obvious: for federal regulators to mandate radically shorter deadlines on utilities to connect data centers to the grid, and potentially to block state measures meant to ensure that data centers pay the full costs of their grid connections and wires usage. The proposal is bad policy and even worse politics.

We all live life online now, so we are all building data centers. We all stand to benefit from the promise of AI. But there are real costs that must be honestly calculated and fully allocated to the cost causers. Soundbites about “winning the AI race with China” will not assuage voters angry about skyrocketing power bills. Indeed, to the extent AI is essential to national security, defense-related AI should be funded the same way the U.S. has always funded advanced military technologies, with tax dollars, not through power bills paid by consumers.

The tech bros’ mantra of “move fast and break things” may work in Silicon Valley, but not in the power grid. Electric utilities and their regulators have an obligation to serve the entire public and the entire economy, not just one industry, no matter how promising. And in a volatile election year, being on the wrong side of a growing grass-roots backlash is dangerous.

Mark Christie directs the Center for Energy Law and Policy at William and Mary Law School, where he teaches energy law. He is a former Chairman of the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission and served 17 years as a utility regulator in Virginia.

data.title: Chilling bodycam video shows California gunfight that killed officer, innocent father
data.description:
(KTLA) — Dramatic body-worn camera footage of a deadly shootout last year in Baldwin Park, Calif., shows the moment a police officer was killed and another wounded in an incident that also left an innocent bystander dead.

The deadly encounter began around 7:15 p.m. on May 31, in a neighborhood where police responded to reports of a man firing a rifle in the street.

An arriving Baldwin Park Police Department officer who was first on scene saw an unresponsive person lying on the ground. Before he could help, however, he immediately began taking fire from an assault-style rifle wielded by the suspect, identified as 22-year-old Eduardo Robert Medina-Berumen.

The suspect also fired at a second patrol unit that arrived moments later, hitting the vehicle twice. One of the shots hit officer Anthony Pimentel’s windshield, sending glass and shrapnel into his face.

Surveillance and witness video showed neighbors, many of whom had gathered for weekend parties and get-togethers, scrambling for safety as rapid gunfire echoed through the neighborhood.

A law enforcement helicopter helped ground units find Medina-Berumen lying in the front yard of a nearby home.

Deputies with the Los Angeles County Sheriff’s Department arrived along with additional Baldwin Park police officers, including 35-year-old Samuel Riveros, a nine-year veteran of the department, who began a dangerous approach toward the suspect.

Riveros was shot while across the street from the shooter’s position.

A team of officers rushed to his aid and dragged him out of the line of fire. He was airlifted to USC Medical Center, where he later died from his injuries.

Officer Pimentel was hospitalized in stable condition and was expected to recover.

The bystander, 43-year-old Darius Wong, a father of two young daughters, was shot in the back. He was also rushed to the hospital, where he was pronounced dead.

Using an armored vehicle, police and deputies positioned themselves in front of the yard where Medina-Berumen had taken cover and shot him when he allegedly reached for the rifle he had been using.

He was transported to the hospital in stable condition and later charged by the Los Angeles County District Attorney’s Office with two counts of murder, one count of attempted murder and possession of an assault weapon.

If convicted, he faces the death penalty or life in prison without the possibility of parole.

“Sam was a dedicated officer who represented our department with dignity and honor,” Baldwin Park Police Chief Robert A. López said of Riveros. “In the face of danger, Sam selflessly put the safety of others before his own and gave his life doing so. We will never forget Sam, and we will lean on one another in our shared grief as we move forward.”

Footage of the deadly gunfight was released by the Baldwin Park Police Department.
